title: Durable Channels, Conversations, and Exactly-Once Semantics
intention:
When this cluster is complete, a developer can declare a channel as durable and get message persistence, replay from offset, and crash recovery without changing their publish/subscribe code. A developer building complex processing can choose a durable conversation and get exactly-once semantics that are emergent from resumable processing — not from delivery guarantees and not from application-level idempotency boilerplate.

The durability layer should feel like a channel property, not a separate system. Setting a channel to durable adds persistence; setting it to ephemeral bypasses haematite entirely with zero overhead. The two exactly-once strategies (lightweight dedup contract and heavyweight durable conversations) are complementary tools chosen per-channel, not competing approaches forced on all consumers.

The bus's relationship with haematite is direct and mechanical: append for writes, cas for cursors, read for replay. No ORM, no query builder, no abstraction that hides what haematite is doing. Developers who understand haematite's append/read/cas model understand the durability layer.
problem:
Ephemeral channels lose all messages on process crash or node restart. Any consumer that was mid-processing loses its progress. There is no way to replay missed messages or resume from a known offset. For simple consumers, the only path to exactly-once is application-level idempotency — every consumer must implement its own dedup logic. For complex consumers (multi-step processing, saga coordination, workflow activities), application-level idempotency is insufficient because re-execution of side effects is not safe.

Without a durability layer, the bus is limited to fire-and-forget use cases. Aion cannot route durable workflow activities through the bus. Operators cannot recover from crashes without message loss. The layer cake (haematite storage, agora messaging, Aion workflows) has a gap between storage and messaging that this cluster fills.
solution:
The durability cluster adds four components to the bus, all built on haematite's EventStore API.

1. Durable Channel Storage

A durable channel persists every published message via haematite's append operation: append(channel_id + partition, message_envelope, expected_seq). The channel actor holds a reference to the haematite store and appends synchronously before acknowledging the publish. Messages are stored as serialised envelopes containing the payload, causal context, timestamp, and publisher identity. Partitioning follows the channel's partition key (defaulting to a single partition). Each partition is an independent append-only log with its own sequence numbers.

Ephemeral channels bypass this entirely — the channel actor delivers directly to subscriber mailboxes with no haematite interaction. The durability mode is set at channel creation and is immutable for the channel's lifetime.

2. Consumer Cursors

Each consumer subscription to a durable channel maintains a cursor — its current read position in the partition log. Cursors are persisted via haematite's cas operation: cas(consumer_id + partition, old_offset, new_offset). The cas ensures that concurrent checkpoint attempts (from retries or restarts) do not regress the cursor.

A consumer can checkpoint at any time (after processing each message, after batches, or on explicit flush). On restart, the consumer reads its persisted cursor and resumes from that offset. Messages between the cursor and the partition head are replayed in sequence order.

Consumer groups share cursor semantics: each member maintains its own cursor for its assigned partition range.

3. Dedup Contract (Lightweight Exactly-Once)

The lightweight strategy implements a three-party contract per ADR-006. The producer provides an idempotency key with each message. The bus maintains a dedup cache mapping idempotency keys to processing receipts, with a configurable TTL. Before delivering a message, the bus checks the cache — if the key exists and has a receipt, delivery is skipped and the existing receipt is returned to the producer.

The consumer, after processing, provides a processing receipt (an opaque bytes value representing the processing outcome, such as a side-effect identifier or output hash). The bus stores this receipt against the idempotency key. The receipt allows the producer to distinguish 'not yet processed' from 'already processed with this outcome.'

The dedup cache is backed by haematite: append(dedup_namespace + key_hash, receipt, expected_seq). TTL expiry is handled by a background sweep that removes entries older than the configured window.

4. Durable Conversations (Heavyweight Exactly-Once)

The heavyweight strategy implements event-sourced conversation state per ADR-006. A durable conversation persists every state transition as an event via append(conversation_id, event, expected_seq). Events include: message received, processing started, step completed (with output), processing finished, error occurred.

On crash and restart, the conversation actor replays its event log from haematite to reconstruct its state. Because every step's completion is recorded, re-delivery of a message that was already fully processed results in a no-op — the conversation detects the duplicate via its event history and skips to the next unprocessed message. Exactly-once is emergent: make your processing resumable and idempotency is free.

The conversation's expected_seq on each append acts as an optimistic concurrency check — if two instances of the same conversation attempt to write (split-brain during failover), only one succeeds and the other detects the conflict.

Integration Surface

The durability module exposes a DurableStore trait that the channel actor calls for persistence operations. The trait maps directly to haematite's API: append, read_from, cas, and scan. A single HaematiteStore implementation backs all durable channels and conversations in a bus instance. The store is injected at bus construction — durable channels receive it; ephemeral channels do not.

Configuration is per-channel: durability mode (ephemeral, durable, durable-dedup, durable-conversation), partition count, dedup TTL, and cursor checkpoint policy. The channel registry enforces that durable channels have a store reference and that ephemeral channels never touch one.

principles.text: Durability is a channel property, not a system mode. Ephemeral and durable channels coexist in the same bus instance with no cross-contamination.
Haematite operations are visible, not abstracted. The durability layer uses append, read, and cas directly. No query language, no ORM, no indirection that hides the storage model.

Exactly-once is not a delivery guarantee. It is either a dedup contract (lightweight) or an emergent property of resumable processing (heavyweight). Consumers choose the strategy that fits their complexity.

Crash recovery is replay, not reconstruction. Durable conversations replay their event log. Cursors replay from their checkpoint. No separate recovery codepath — the normal read path is the recovery path.

Sequence numbers are the concurrency control. Haematite's expected_seq on append and cas's compare-and-swap are the only concurrency primitives. No locks, no transactions, no coordination beyond what the storage API provides.
Ephemeral channels pay nothing. No haematite reference, no store allocation, no durability bookkeeping. The absence of durability is not a degenerate case of its presence.

goals: A durable channel persists every published message to haematite and survives process restart without message loss.
A consumer can checkpoint its cursor, crash, restart, and resume processing from the checkpointed offset with no duplicate deliveries beyond the checkpoint.
The dedup contract prevents duplicate processing of a message when the producer provides an idempotency key, the bus caches the key, and the consumer provides a processing receipt.
A durable conversation replays its event log on restart and skips already-processed messages without re-executing side effects.
Ephemeral channels operate with zero haematite interaction — no store reference, no append calls, no cursor tracking.
All durability operations use haematite's append/read/cas API with no additional concurrency primitives.

non_goals.reason: Each partition is an independent log. Cross-partition atomicity would require a transaction coordinator, which conflicts with haematite's single-writer-per-shard model.
Deferred to an operational cluster. The durability layer appends and reads; retention policy is a separate concern.
Routing functions and predicates are stateless or rely on the routing cluster's own persistence. The durability cluster persists messages and processing state, not routing decisions.
Replication depends on haematite's sync protocol and the distribution cluster. This cluster handles single-node durability only.
Schema evolution is a channel-level concern handled by the core cluster's type system. The durability layer stores and retrieves opaque envelopes.

structure.note: Module root: pub mod declarations and pub use re-exports only
DurabilityError enum and From conversions for haematite errors
Per-channel durability configuration: mode, partition count, dedup TTL, checkpoint policy
DurableStore trait and HaematiteStore implementation bridging to haematite's EventStore API
Durable channel storage: message append, partition management, sequence tracking
Consumer cursor: cas-based offset checkpointing, restart-from-offset
Message replay: read-from-offset, range iteration, cursor-driven replay loop
Idempotency key cache: key-to-receipt mapping, TTL enforcement, background sweep
Processing receipt types: receipt creation, storage, and lookup
Durable conversation: event-sourced state, event append, replay-on-restart, duplicate detection
Crash recovery: event log replay, cursor reconstruction, conversation state rebuild

constraints.text: No file over 500 lines. If a module approaches this limit, extract into submodules.
No panics in production code. .unwrap() and .expect() only in tests.
mod.rs contains ONLY pub mod declarations and pub use re-exports. No logic, no type definitions, no function bodies.
All public types implement Debug. All error types implement std::error::Error via thiserror.
Every haematite append call must include an expected_seq for optimistic concurrency. No blind appends.
Consumer cursor checkpoint must use cas (compare-and-swap), never unconditional write. A stale checkpoint must fail, not silently regress.
Durable conversation replay must be idempotent: replaying the same event log twice produces identical state.
Ephemeral channels must not hold a reference to the DurableStore. The type system must prevent accidental persistence calls on ephemeral channels.
Dedup TTL expiry must never delete a receipt that is younger than the configured TTL, even under clock skew up to 1 second.
All durability operations must propagate haematite errors as DurabilityError variants, never swallow or log-and-continue.
